groups.name: Justified universe
groups.explanation: Raylan Givens (protagonist), Boyd Crowder (villain/antihero), Harlan County (setting), and 'We dug coal together' (iconic quote) are all from the FX series Justified.

groups.name: Shows That Got a Revival or Continuation Years After Cancellation
groups.explanation: N/A ended in 2020 but its universe was revived in comics/audio; Arrested Development was revived on Netflix after years off air; Unbreakable Kimmy Schmidt got a Netflix interactive special years after the show ended; Firefly was famously cancelled then continued as the film Serenity plus comics. All four properties received notable continuations well after their original run ended.

groups.name: TV Shows Whose Entire Final Scene Was Revealed to Be Someone's Imagination or Dream
groups.explanation: The Sopranos (Bob Hartley (Newhart)) ended with a widely theorized 'death in his mind' cut to black; Newhart's finale revealed the entire show was a dream of Bob Hartley; N/A's finale used a visionary flash-forward sequence framed as Claire's imagination; St. Elsewhere's finale revealed the entire series existed in an autistic boy's snow globe fantasy. All four finales are defined by a reality-questioning final reveal.

groups.name: Characters Whose Name Became the Show's Title — But They Never Appear On Screen
groups.explanation: Players assume these are simply famous TV characters — but the trap is this: Fleabag's protagonist is never given her real name on screen (she is only ever 'Fleabag'); Nurse Ratched — in the Netflix prequel Ratched, the character exists before she becomes 'Nurse Ratched' so the titular identity never fully appears; Dexter Morgan is the title character of Dexter but in Dexter: New Blood the character operates entirely under the alias 'Jim Lindsay' and 'Dexter' is never spoken aloud by anyone who knows him for almost the entire run; Don Draper is not his real name — the actual Don Draper never appears, and the real man (Dick Whitman) effectively means the title character is a ghost. All four are title-adjacent characters who are defined by an identity that is absent, hidden, or never fully seen.
